does: RewardHarness introduces a self-evolving agentic reward framework for evaluating instruction-guided image edits that operates without traditional weight optimization or large-scale training. The system maintains a dynamically updated library of tools and skills that an Orchestrator selects from based on specific editing tasks, while a frozen Sub-Agent constructs reasoning chains to produce preference judgments. The framework evolves this library iteratively from as few as 100 preference demonstrations by analyzing successes and failures, achieving data efficiency comparable to human learning while adapting to new evaluation criteria through tool and skill composition rather than model retraining.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in in-context learning and agent-based reasoning that enable models to perform complex tasks through tool use and prompt engineering rather than parameter updates. It extends prior reward modeling research that has struggled with data efficiency and generalization, traditionally requiring hundreds of thousands of annotated comparisons. The approach connects to broader work on agentic systems and evolutionary optimization in prompting, as well as research on compositional reasoning in language models. It responds to the gap between human efficiency in preference learning and model requirements identified in contemporary preference modeling literature, while drawing on techniques from prompt-based in-context learning that avoid expensive fine-tuning.
enables: RewardHarness opens pathways for developing reward models that adapt rapidly to new domains and tasks with minimal human annotation, potentially extending to other domains beyond image editing where subtle preference judgments matter. The self-evolving tool library approach suggests future work on scaling agentic frameworks for multi-task reward modeling and automatic capability discovery. This framework could enable interactive and iterative deployment of reward systems that learn from user feedback in real-time without model retraining, supporting applications in personalized content generation, design systems, and interactive creative tools. The separation of reasoning from parameter learning also points toward more interpretable and modifiable reward systems where human preferences can be encoded through tool composition rather than black-box weights.
